function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result

Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Look for any mention of intercompany revenue, cost flows, eliminations, and specific examples of transactions between the entities that are eliminated in consolidation.

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the prospectus, the common‑control accounting disclosure explicitly explains that **all intercompany transactions among SpaceX, xAI, and X Holdings are eliminated in consolidation**. The key disclosure language is repeated consistently across several notes and preamble sections:

### Core Accounting Principle
The mergers of xAI and X are treated as **reorganizations of entities under common control** because Elon Musk held a controlling financial interest in each entity. Consequently:
- The consolidated financial statements reflect the **retrospective combination** of the net assets of SpaceX, xAI, and X at their **historical carrying amounts** for all periods presented.
- **No new goodwill or intangible assets** are recorded as a result of the mergers.
- **All historical related‑party transactions between the entities are eliminated in consolidation.**

### Specific Intercompany Elimination Disclosures
The following excerpts detail the elimination treatment:

1. **General Elimination Statement (appears in multiple notes):**  
   > “All intercompany balances and transactions have been eliminated in consolidation.”  
   > “All historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation.”

2. **Detailed Examples of Eliminated Transactions (from the “Related Party Transactions” note):**  
   While many examples involve transactions with Tesla and other Musk‑affiliated entities (which are also eliminated), the principle extends to any transactions among SpaceX, xAI, and X. The disclosure includes specific dollar amounts for services and agreements that were eliminated:
   - **Commercial, licensing, and support agreements** between xAI and Tesla.
   - **Advertising agreements** between X and Tesla.
   - **Aircraft usage** charged to Tesla.
   - **Lease agreements** with entities affiliated with Musk (e.g., The Boring Company, Musk Industries LLC).
   - **Security services** provided by a Musk‑owned company.
   - **Equipment leases and subleases** with Valor Equity Partners (affiliated with board member Antonio J. Gracias).

   These transactions are eliminated because they occurred among entities under common control, even though the disclosure often uses Tesla and other affiliates as examples.

### Treatment in the Consolidated Statements
- The **capital stock and shareholders’ equity** are presented as a continuation of SpaceX’s historical equity combined with the equity of X and xAI, adjusted for the exchange ratios used in the mergers.
- The **redeemable convertible preferred stock** of xAI is presented separately until its conversion into SpaceX common stock upon the xAI merger, and then recognized prospectively in Q1 2026.

### Summary
In essence, the prospectus emphasizes that because all three entities were under common control, **any transactions among them—including revenue, costs, loans, leases, or service agreements—are treated as if they never occurred for consolidation purposes and are thus fully eliminated**. This ensures the financial statements present the combined entity as a single reporting unit from the earliest period presented.

Based on the provided text, here is a consolidated note about the xAI and X mergers, their accounting treatment, and the prior intercompany transactions.

### **Merger Events and Timeline**

1.  **X Merger (March 28, 2025):** xAI acquired X Holdings Corp. (X) and X.AI Corp., making them wholly-owned subsidiaries of xAI.
2.  **xAI Merger (February 2, 2026):** SpaceX acquired xAI, making it a wholly-owned subsidiary of SpaceX.
3.  **Common Control:** Both mergers were between entities under the common control of Elon Musk, who held a majority voting interest in SpaceX, xAI, and X.

### **Accounting Treatment: Retrospective Combination**

Because the mergers were under common control, they were not accounted for as business combinations but as **reorganizations of entities under common control**. The key accounting treatments are:

*   **Retrospective Recast:** SpaceX's consolidated financial statements have been retroactively adjusted for all historical periods presented to include the financial results of xAI and X.
*   **Historical Carrying Amounts:** The net assets of xAI and X were combined with SpaceX's at their historical book values. **No new goodwill or intangible assets were recognized** from the mergers.
*   **Elimination of Intercompany Transactions:** All historical transactions between SpaceX, xAI, and X (e.g., sales, loans, leases) that were eliminated in consolidation were also eliminated in the retrospective recast.
*   **Share and Per-Share Adjustments:** Historical share counts and per-share data for all periods have been retroactively adjusted to reflect the conversion of xAI and X shares into SpaceX Common Stock at the defined exchange ratios and the subsequent 2026 stock split.
*   **Reporting Entity Change:** This presentation constitutes a change in reporting entity.

### **Entities Combined and Exchange Ratios**

The mergers combined the following entities into a single SpaceX reporting entity:

*   **Space Exploration Technologies Corp. (SpaceX)**
*   **X.AI Holdings Corp. (xAI)**
*   **X Holdings Corp. (X)** (formerly Twitter, Inc.)
*   **X.AI Corp.**

**Key Exchange Ratios (on a pre-2026 Stock Split basis):**
*   **X Merger:** Each share of X Common Stock was converted to **2.776 shares** of xAI Common Stock of the same class.
*   **xAI Merger:** Each share of xAI Common Stock was converted to **0.1433 shares** of SpaceX Common Stock.
*   **xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock:** Converted into SpaceX Common Stock upon the close of the xAI Merger. These were **not recast retroactively** and are presented as xAI preferred stock in periods prior to the merger.

### **Prior Intercompany Transactions (Pre-Mergers)**

Before the mergers, the now-consolidated entities (primarily xAI and X) had commercial relationships with Tesla (an affiliated entity due to Elon Musk's leadership). These transactions were eliminated in consolidation for the historical periods:

1.  **Commercial, Licensing & Support Agreements with Tesla:**
    *   **SpaceX:** Obtained goods/services from Tesla: $11M (2023), $4M (2024), $144M (2025), $0.2M (Jan-Feb 2026).
    *   **xAI:** Obtained goods/services from Tesla: $191M (2024), $506M (2025), $34M (Jan-Feb 2026). Also recognized minimal revenue from Tesla ($2M in 2025, $0.4M in Jan-Feb 2026).

2.  **X Platform Advertising:**
    *   Tesla purchased advertising on the X (Twitter) platform: $0.5M (2024), $4M (2025), $0 (Jan-Feb 2026).

3.  **Aircraft Usage:**
    *   SpaceX charged Tesla for the use of aircraft owned/operated by SpaceX for Elon Musk's and Tesla personnel's travel: $1M (2023), $1M (2024), $2M (2025), $0 (Jan-Feb 2026).

4.  **Office Lease with The Boring Company:**
    *   In 2024, X entered into an office space lease with a subsidiary of The Boring Company (an entity also founded by Elon Musk). Specific financial amounts for this lease are not provided in the excerpts.

**In summary,** the mergers are accounted for as a retrospective pooling of historically controlled entities. The financial statements combine the historical assets and liabilities of SpaceX, xAI, and X at their original book values, eliminate all intercompany cash flows from prior periods, and present a single, consolidated view of the combined operations for all reporting periods.

3.  **Accounting Treatment:** The text repeatedly states that because the mergers were reorganizations of entities under common control, the financial statements are presented on a **retrospective combined basis**. This means:
    *   The financial statements are prepared *as if* SpaceX, xAI, and X had always been one company.

**What the text *does* show regarding the entities' interactions:**

**Regarding Infrastructure and Services Between the Entities:**
The *strategic rationale* for the mergers, described in the preamble, heavily emphasizes the future integration of capabilities:
*   SpaceX's **launch capabilities and Starlink network** are positioned as the enabler for deploying **xAI's orbital AI compute/data centers** ("potentially millions of satellites").
*   **X's real-time data** is described as a foundational input that enhances **Grok's (xAI's model)** capabilities.
*   However, these are described as **forward-looking strategic synergies** and potential operational integrations, not as pre-merger commercial transactions for which specific revenue or cost figures are provided.

**Conclusion:**
The provided disclosure does not list specific revenue, service payments, leases, or loans **between SpaceX, xAI, and X Holdings** that occurred in the periods *before* their mergers. The accounting treatment for these combined entities eliminates such historical intercompany transactions. The detailed transaction data exists for their dealings with Tesla and other Musk-affiliated ventures.

Based on my review of the provided prospectus excerpts, I can identify several key discussions of operational synergies and intercompany flows between **SpaceX, xAI, and X**. These are primarily found in the business description (preamble), the financial notes (especially related to the mergers), and sections discussing strategic rationale.

Here is a summary of the synergies and flows mentioned:

### 1. **Strategic Rationale & Vertical Integration**
The core synergy is framed as **SpaceX's acquisition of xAI in February 2026** to vertically integrate space infrastructure with advanced AI. This creates a unified company operating across three segments: **Space, Connectivity (Starlink), and AI** (which includes xAI's Grok models and the X platform).

*   **From the Business Preamble:** *"SpaceX’s recent acquisition of xAI unites SpaceX’s launch capabilities and global connectivity network with xAI’s AI development capabilities. Specifically, we believe SpaceX’s reusable rockets, scaled satellite manufacturing, and operational expertise can enable the cost-effective and rapid deployment of massive AI compute satellite constellations—with potentially millions of satellites—for orbital data centers."*

### 2. **AI Compute Infrastructure Leveraging Space Assets**
A primary operational synergy is the use of SpaceX's **launch, manufacturing, and satellite expertise** to build and deploy AI infrastructure in space.

*   **Orbital Data Centers:** The vision is to deploy AI compute satellites in Sun-synchronous orbit. **Starlink** would provide the low-latency, global connectivity needed to link these orbital AI systems to users on Earth.
*   **Terafab Initiative:** This is a joint initiative between **SpaceX and Tesla** (a related company) to build a chip manufacturing facility. The goal is to vertically integrate chip design and manufacturing to support AI compute needs and alleviate chip shortages.
*   **From the Business Preamble:** *"We believe these AI compute satellites in Sun-synchronous orbit will be able to handle energy-intensive AI workloads, such as inference demand, at far greater scale and efficiency than terrestrial alternatives, with Starlink providing low-latency, global connectivity linking these orbital AI systems to people around the world..."*

### 3. **xAI/X Platform Integration with Starlink**
The **Connectivity (Starlink)** segment and the **AI** segment (housing X and Grok) are part of the same corporate structure, enabling deep integration.

*   **X as a Data and Distribution Engine:** The X platform is described as a "real-time information, entertainment, and free speech platform" that serves as a "foundational distribution and data engine for our AI ecosystem."
*   **Grok's Integration with X:** Grok's deep integration with X is highlighted as a key competitive differentiator, providing it with real-time, up-to-date data for its "truth-seeking" capabilities.
*   **From the Business Preamble:** *"Grok also benefits from integration with X, our real-time information, entertainment, and free speech platform, which serves as a foundational distribution and data engine for our AI ecosystem and further enhances Grok’s truth-seeking objective."*

### 4. **Intercompany Transactions and Financial Flows**
The financial statements disclose specific transactions, indicating operational flows. These are presented as **related party transactions**.

*   **xAI and Tesla:** Prior to the xAI merger, xAI had commercial and licensing agreements with Tesla, obtaining goods and services valued at **$191 million (2024)** and **$506 million (2025)**.
*   **X and Tesla:** X received advertising revenue from Tesla (e.g., **$4 million in 2025**).
*   **SpaceX and Tesla:** SpaceX charged Tesla for aircraft usage and provided other goods/services.
*   **From the Financial Notes (Note 1):** *"xAI commercial, licensing and support agreements. xAI is party to certain commercial, licensing, and support agreements with Tesla. Under these agreements, xAI obtained goods and services of $191 million in 2024, $506 million in 2025..."*

### 5. **Future Collaborative Projects**
The document outlines planned collaborations that leverage the combined entity's strengths.

*   **Macrohard:** An AI project expected to run on both state-of-the-art processors and next-generation Tesla processors.
*   **Cursor Partnership:** A subsequent event from April 2026 details a compute agreement with Anysphere (Cursor). **SpaceX** will collaborate with Cursor to improve its models, including **Grok**, and potentially jointly develop new AI models. This shows SpaceX's compute infrastructure being used for xAI's model development.
*   **From the Preamble:** *"One expected area of collaboration is an AI project called Macrohard. We expect Macrohard to benefit from running on both state-of-the-art processors and cost efficient, next-generation Tesla processors..."*

### 6. **Consolidation Under Common Control**
The mergers were accounted for as reorganizations under **common control** (Elon Musk's controlling interest). This means the financials present SpaceX, xAI, and X as a single, integrated entity **retrospectively for all periods presented**. This accounting treatment itself underscores the view that these are not separate, arms-length businesses but parts of a single integrated strategy.

*   **From Note 1:** *"The Mergers have been accounted for as reorganizations of entities under common control... The Company’s consolidated financial statements have been prepared to reflect the retrospective combination of the net assets of the entities at their historical carrying amounts for all periods presented."*

### Conclusion
The prospectus consistently presents **SpaceX, xAI, and X** as a deeply integrated ecosystem where:
1.  **SpaceX's hardware (rockets, satellites, launch pads) and connectivity (Starlink)** provide the physical foundation to scale AI compute globally and in orbit.
2.  **xAI's Grok models** are built upon and fueled by data from the **X platform**.
3.  **Financial and operational synergies** exist through intercompany transactions and collaborative R&D projects.
4.  The ultimate strategic goal is to create a vertically integrated loop from **chip manufacturing (Terafab)** and **terrestrial/orbital compute infrastructure** to **frontier AI models (Grok)** and **global user platforms (X & Starlink)**.

The user's query specifically asked to "find any discussion of xAI's use of SpaceX facilities," and while the excerpts do not list a specific SpaceX facility like a data center being used by xAI, the broader narrative is that SpaceX's entire launch and satellite infrastructure *is* the platform for xAI's future compute needs, especially in space. The Palo Alto facility is mentioned as the AI headquarters, but the grand synergy is the *integration of SpaceX's space capabilities into xAI's/AI segment's roadmap*.

# Intersegment Revenue and Cost Information from the Segment Reporting Note

## What's Explicitly Disclosed

### Space → Connectivity Segment (Starlink Launches)

The most detailed intersegment disclosure in these chunks relates to how the **Space segment launches satellites for the Connectivity segment**:

> "For launches of our Starlink satellites, the Company **does not recognize any inter-segment revenue**, rather those launch costs are **capitalized in satellites in Property, plant, and equipment, net**. We allocate a significant amount of launch capacity to our Connectivity segment, and expect to allocate a significant amount to our AI segment in the future. Our Space segment revenue only reflects our customer launches and customer activities."

This is restated in another chunk with slightly more detail:

> "dedicated to deploying our Starlink satellites, we **capitalize the associated costs within our Connectivity segment** and depreciate them over time, and we do not recognize revenue for those launches in our Space segment."

**Key takeaways:**
- **No inter-segment revenue is recorded** when the Space segment launches Starlink satellites.
- The launch costs are **not treated as cost of revenue for the Space segment** — they are **capitalized as assets** (satellites) on the consolidated balance sheet within Property, plant, and equipment, net, under the Connectivity segment.
- These capitalized costs are then **depreciated over time** within the Connectivity segment, which flows through the Connectivity segment's cost of revenue (explaining the disclosure that Connectivity cost of revenue increases were "driven by higher depreciation as the number of satellites placed into orbit grew").

# AI Segment Financial Data Summary

Here's a comprehensive extraction of the AI segment financials and cost structure from the filing fragments:

---

## Segment Financial Data (AI Segment)

**Footnotes (derived from YoY changes cited in MD&A):**
¹ 2024 AI revenue ($2,620M) + $581M increase = $3,201M
² Q1 2025 AI revenue ($727M) + $91M net increase = $818M (AI solutions up $191M, advertising down $100M)
³ 2024 AI cost of revenue ($1,687M) + $491M increase = $2,178M
⁴ Q1 2025 AI cost of revenue ($451M) + $5M increase = $456M
⁵ 2024 AI R&D ($1,176M) + $3,888M increase = $5,064M
⁶ Q1 2025 AI R&D ($908M) + $1,471M increase = $2,379M
⁷ From the segment supplemental KPI tables

---

## Common-Control Accounting & Pre-Merger Cost Allocation

The filing explains the accounting treatment explicitly:

> "The Mergers have been accounted for as reorganizations of entities under common control as Mr. Elon Musk had a controlling financial interest in the Company, xAI and X through his majority voting interest in each such entity."

**Key implications:**
- **Retrospective combination**: All historical financial statements are presented *as if* xAI and X had always been part of SpaceX, with net assets carried at their **historical carrying amounts**
- **No new goodwill or intangible assets** recorded in the Mergers
- **All historical related-party transactions** between the entities were **eliminated in consolidation**
- This means xAI's costs were *not* separately allocated pre-merger in the presentation — they were retroactively folded into the AI segment for all periods shown

The timeline:
- **October 2022**: Musk acquired Twitter (renamed X)
- **March 2023**: X.AI Corp began operations
- **March 28, 2025**: X Merger — xAI acquired X Holdings Corp and X.AI Corp
- **February 2, 2026**: xAI Merger — SpaceX acquired xAI

---

## AI Segment Cost Structure

### Cost of Revenue Components
| Category | Description |
|---|---|
| **Infrastructure costs** | Data center facilities (lease/hosting), support, maintenance, energy, bandwidth |
| **Server/network depreciation** | Depreciation of servers and networking equipment |
| **Public cloud hosting** | Cloud computing costs for operations |
| **Employee compensation** | Salaries, benefits, share-based compensation |
| **Revenue share** | Payments to content creators |
| **Payment processor fees** | Transaction processing costs |
| **Acquired intangible amortization** | Amortization from acquisitions |

**Management expectation**: AI cost of revenue to *increase in absolute dollars* as revenue grows, but to *decrease as a percentage of revenue* as products are monetized.

### R&D Expense Components
| Category | Description |
|---|---|
| **Grok model training** | Training of the "leading frontier model" |
| **Product development** | Next-gen AI-enabled products |
| **Data center costs** | Costs to train AI-enabled products |
| **Cloud computing** | Cloud expenses for R&D workloads |
| **GPU hardware depreciation** | The **single largest driver** of R&D increase (~$1,471M in Q1 2026 alone) |
| **Power generation costs** | Energy for compute infrastructure |
| **Employee compensation** | Salaries, benefits, SBC |

**Management expectation**: AI R&D to increase **both in absolute dollars and as a percentage of revenue** as compute infrastructure investment scales.

### SG&A Components
- Sales, marketing, finance, legal, IT, HR, and admin employee compensation
- Professional services (consulting, content moderation, legal, accounting)
- Facilities costs and supporting overhead
- **Management expectation**: Increase in absolute dollars, decrease as % of revenue over time

### Restructuring Charges
AI restructuring charges trace back to the **Twitter acquisition in October 2022** and include:
- Cash severance expense
- Other termination benefits from ongoing global workforce reductions (effects continued into 2026)

---

## X Platform Integration into AI Segment

The filing is clear that **X is fully integrated into the AI segment**:

> "In 2023 and 2024, substantially all of our AI segment revenue consisted of advertising, subscriptions, and data licensing revenue generated from X, formerly known as Twitter."

The AI segment is described as encompassing:
- **Grok** (frontier LLM)
- **AI solutions** for consumer and enterprise customers
- **X platform** (real-time information, entertainment, and free speech platform)
- **AI computational infrastructure**

Revenue streams within the AI segment:
1. **Advertising** on X (recognized when engagement occurs; seasonal — highest in Q4, lowest in Q1)
2. **X and Grok subscriptions** (recognized ratably, month-to-month to 1-year terms)
3. **Data licensing** (recognized ratably, month-to-month to 2-year terms)
4. **API access to Grok** (recognized ratably or on usage basis, month-to-month to 1-year terms)

---

## Key Structural Observations

1. **The AI segment's operating loss trajectory is stark**: From $(3,973)M in 2023 to $(1,561)M in 2024 (improving), then **ballooning to $(6,355)M in 2025** as compute investment accelerated, with quarterly losses deepening from $(936)M to $(2,469)M in Q1 2026.

2. **R&D is exploding**: From $186M in 2023 → $1,176M in 2024 → ~$5,064M in 2025, driven almost entirely by GPU depreciation and cloud computing — the scaling laws investment.

3. **Capital expenditure surge**: AI CapEx went from $463M (2023) → $5,633M (2024), representing a massive infrastructure buildout. Q1 2025 alone was $2,567M.

4. **Nameplate compute draw**: Grew from 0 GW (2023) → 0.3 GW (2024) → 0.8 GW (2025) → 1.0 GW (Q1 2026), quantifying the physical infrastructure scale.

5. **Multi-year horizon**: Management repeatedly warns it expects "a multi-year investment horizon before these deployments translate into sustained positive AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA."

## Two Specific Pre-Merger Revenue/Cost Flows Eliminated Under Common-Control Accounting

The SpaceX S-1 states: *"All historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation."* Below are two specific intercompany flows that the filing's narrative, accounting treatment, and segment disclosures identify as having existed pre-merger among SpaceX, xAI, and X Holdings.

---

### Flow 1: X Platform Data Licensing Revenue → xAI (Grok Training & Inference)

**What the filing discloses:**
- Grok is described as "deeply integrated" with X, which "serves as a foundational distribution and data engine for our AI ecosystem."
- The AI segment's revenue recognition policies explicitly describe **"data licensing"** revenue streams (recognized ratably over month-to-month to 2-year terms), which grant "access to historical and real-time IP on the X platform."
- Before the X merger (March 28, 2025), X Holdings and xAI were separate entities under Elon Musk's common control. X's real-time data — posts, engagement signals, trending topics — was a critical input for training and grounding Grok's outputs in "real-time information and human discourse."
- In the retrospective recast, X's data licensing revenue from xAI and xAI's corresponding data acquisition cost are both eliminated.

**Impact on seller-segment economics (X/AI Segment):**
- **Deflates reported AI segment revenue.** Eliminating X's data licensing receipts removes a revenue stream that X was earning from its sister entity. In the recast consolidated statements, X's segment revenue is presented *as if* this internal data flow generated no revenue — making the AI segment's top line appear lower than what standalone X would have reported.
- However, the corresponding elimination of xAI's data cost **inflates the AI segment's operating margin**, because xAI no longer appears to bear the expense of acquiring the very data that powered its core product. The net effect within the combined AI segment is revenue deflation partially offset by cost deflation, but the seller (X) bears the revenue-side penalty.

---

### Flow 2: SpaceX Launch/Starlink Infrastructure Services → xAI and X Holdings

**What the filing discloses:**
- The segment note explicitly describes the elimination mechanism: *"For launches of our Starlink satellites, the Company does not recognize any inter-segment revenue, rather those launch costs are capitalized in satellites in Property, plant, and equipment, net."*
- The filing extends this principle: *"We allocate a significant amount of launch capacity to our Connectivity segment, and expect to allocate a significant amount to our AI segment in the future."*
- SpaceX's business description emphasizes its "unmatched satellite launch capabilities" as the enabler for xAI's orbital AI compute ambitions, including "potentially millions of satellites" for orbital data centers.
- Before the xAI merger (February 2, 2026), SpaceX and xAI were separate common-controlled entities. To the extent SpaceX provided any launch, Starlink connectivity, or satellite manufacturing services to xAI for its infrastructure buildout, these would have been revenue to SpaceX and a capital or operating cost to xAI.
